management of critical ill patients. Dr Bear has been awarded British Dietetic Association Roll of Honour three

fostering professional development. Over the last seven years, Dr Bear has provided mentorship supporting my
professional development and research experience, and her ongoing support will be invaluable as I further
develop and expand my professional skillset.
Dr Bear embarked on her career as a clinical academic following completion of an NIHR CDRF in 2020. Her

care.

evidence.text:
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited metabolic condition that leads to the buildup of an amino acid (a building
block of protein) called phenylalanine. All babies born in the UK are tested for PKU at birth. High phenylalanine
levels can cause severe brain damage; however, early treatment prevents this. Treatment is for life, and involves
reducing the amount of protein in the diet (to usually less than 15 grams daily) to limit phenylalanine intake. The
treatment also involves taking medical supplements (called PKU supplements) that contain all amino acids, except
for phenylalanine.
For adults with PKU, we do not know the ideal amount of protein needed each day (from foods and PKU
supplements). We know from studies in healthy adults that dietary protein plays an important role in keeping
muscles healthy. It is important to get the amount of protein right for adults with PKU. Too little protein could lead
to muscle problems later in life, whereas too much protein, through taking more PKU supplements, could make the
diet too hard to follow.
We have planned two studies:
Study 1: Do people with PKU suffer from poor muscle health, and is it related to diet?
We will measure how a PKU diet
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
affects muscle mass (the amount of muscle someone has) and muscle function
(how well the muscle works) in adults with PKU. Participants will be asked to keep a record of their food intake for
four days, and to wear an accelerometer on their thigh, a match box size device that monitors movement, for a
week before visiting a centre for the following tests:
1. Exercises to measure muscle function: jumping, climbing steps and grip strength
2. Muscle mass measurements: a DXA scan (full-body x-ray scan), bioelectrical impedance analysis (similar
to scans used in some gyms, four small stickers are placed on the hands and feet), and skin thickness in
different places on the body using a hand-held device (skinfold caliper)
3. Blood tests to measure levels of different proteins
4. A bloodspot test to measure phenylalanine (similar to a finger prick test for blood sugar)
5. Questionnaires about diet, quality of life, and exercise
Study 2: Does increasing protein intake improve muscle mass and function in PKU?
Participants in Study 1 who are found to have a low muscle function will be invited to join a second study. This
study will see if taking more protein over 12 weeks improves muscle mass
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
and/or function. Half the participants will
take their normal amount of protein, and the other half will be asked to take more protein (through increased PKU
supplements). Everyone will be advised to do regular exercises.
During the 12 weeks, participants will keep a diary to record their diet and exercise routine. They will be asked to
send in a bloodspot test every 14 days, similar to their current clinical care.
After 12 weeks, the participants will repeat the same tests as Study 1. They will also have an ultrasound scan to
measure the size of their arm and thigh muscles at the beginning and end of Study 2.
Finally, participants and Dietitians will be asked to give feedback on the studies to help improve further research.
People with PKU have been involved in the study design and will continue to help with patient information leaflets,
questionnaires, and sharing the results with the PKU community.
We hope that these studies will help design a larger study looking at how diet can be adjusted to improve muscle
mass and function in adults with PKU. The results will also guide Doctors and Dietitians to ensure adults with PKU
maintain good health as they age.

evidence.text:
Background
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a rare inherited metabolic disorder of protein metabolism with a prevalence of 1:10,000
in the UK. Left untreated, high serum phenylalanine causes significant irreversible neurocognitive disability.
Dietary management remains the mainstay of treatment, consisting of lifelong adherence to a very low protein diet
(typically <15 grams per day) and provision of up to 80% of protein intake from phenylalanine-free or low-
phenylalanine protein substitutes9.
1. Problems being addressed
Establishing optimal protein intake in adults with PKU: “Not too little, not too much”
With the onset of newborn screening for PKU in 1969, there is now an increasing adult and ageing PKU
population requiring dietary guidance to support changing metabolic demands across the lifespan. In my narrative
review, I highlighted that there is currently limited evidence informing protein requirement guidelines for adults
with PKU1.
Current protein intake guidance for adults with PKU is extrapolated from healthy population data, using estimates
of protein requirements based on either nitrogen balance (NB) methodology or from younger adults with PKU1.
The limitations associated with

NB methodology are widely recognised10. Utilising Indicator Amino Acid Oxidation
(IAAO) as a more contemporary approach to estimating protein requirements, protein requirements have been
reported to be 39-55% higher in PKU, than recommendations informed by NB methodology11. Increased
consumption of specialised protein substitutes would be required to achieve this higher protein intake; however,
people on a PKU diet report poor palatability of these supplements, and adherence to a PKU diet has been shown
to reduce with age12-14.
The impact of the PKU diet on muscle mass and function, and by extension, risk of cardiometabolic disease and
frailty in later life, is unknown2,15. Given that a PKU diet involves modifying sources of ingested protein,
establishing the optimal protein intake in adults with PKU is essential because an inadequate protein intake will
likely compromise muscle mass and function (encompassing strength, power and mobility), whereas excessive
protein intake will result in a substantial prescribed supplement burden to patients.
2. Importance of research to improving health of service users and health care services in PKU
Adults with PKU are concerned about their mus

mmendations1. There is an immediate need for research specifically for
adults with PKU who, following successful neonatal screening programmes, are for the first time now entering
middle-older adulthood, and are being advised lifelong PKU restricted diets. We must ensure dietary management
leads to optimal health outcomes across the life course.
Relevance
Protein status in adults with PKU
I designed and published the protocol18, and subsequently performed and published the first-ever scoping review
to understand how dietary management translates to body protein status (encompassing anthropometric,
biochemical and functional measures) in people with PKU2. I identified 70 studies that measured relevant
markers; however, only one study assessed protein status as the primary outcome. The majority of studies were
conducted in children and adolescents with PKU, with very few in adults. Taken together, this review highlights a
significant gap in the current evidence and urgent need for research focused on understanding protein status in
adults with PKU.
Relevant outcome measurements
In my scoping review, I identified a small number of studies with mixed findings regarding muscle mass in a

dults
with PKU vs. healthy controls2. The only study to measure muscle function reported 38% of participants with PKU
had grip strength <3rd percentile2,19. Furthermore, pre-albumin (biochemical marker of protein status) was lower in
people with PKU2. Since publishing my scoping review, two recent studies have reported lower muscle mass and
handgrip strength in adults with PKU compared to controls20,21; however, these studies were in a different
healthcare system (Chile and Hungary) and did not address the questions raised in this research proposal. To
date, no study has measured muscle mass, function and biochemical markers of protein status in adults with
PKU, and investigated impacts on QoL. This multiple measurement approach is important for two reasons; first,
because adequate muscle mass does not mean adequate muscle function22 and it is muscle function that is
important to people with PKU, and second, because Dietitians do not know the best measurement method to use
in practice.
Relevant intervention
The optimal protein intake for adults with PKU remains unknown. No intervention studies have investigated the
impact of recommending dietary protein intake based on NB-informed

(standard current practice) vs. IAAO-informed protein requirements (1.2g/kg/day) on muscle mass and function in
adults with PKU. I aim to establish protein requirements for adults with PKU by performing a feasibility study (as
part of my PhD) and then undertaking a larger RCT (as part of a NIHR ACAF).
4. Research aims and objectives
This research proposal aims to:
(i) assess muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters of protein status in adults with PKU;
(ii) investigate whether impairments in muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters in adults with
PKU are associated with PKU dietary management practices;
(iii) examine the feasibility of a study investigating the comparison of protein intake informed by NB (0.83g/kg/day)
vs. IAAO (1.2g/kg/day) and its impact on muscle mass and function, and phenylalanine control in adults with PKU.
Objectives
I propose to undertake two studies:
Study 1: Observational study
 Primary objective: To investigate differences in muscle mass and function (anthropometric, functional and
biochemical measurements) in adults with PKU vs. reference healthy population dataset.
Secondary objective:

To investigate associations between dietary factors (protein intake, protein

substitute use), muscle mass and physical activity, and muscle function in adults with PKU.
Study 2: Feasibility study:
 To determine the impact of increasing prescribed protein intake and exercise training on muscle mass
and function in adults with PKU who have compromised muscle strength.
 To establish the feasibility of implementing this complex intervention over 12 weeks in adults with PKU,
with the view to designing a full-scale RCT.
5. Project Plan
Study 1: Observational study
Study design
A cross-sectional, observational study will be conducted over an 18-month period in adults with PKU enrolled from
the Adult Metabolic Service at GSTT.
An observational study is critical to understanding the association between current dietary management practices
on muscle mass and function in adults with PKU and to establish the most robust endpoints for use in future trials.
The proposed research will be the first to simultaneously measure anthropometric, functional and biochemical
parameters of protein status in an adult UK cohort with PKU.
Methods
Recruitment and consent
Fifty adults with PKU will be recrui

pants in total. With 38% of
adults with PKU previously found to have a grip strength <3rd percentile19, it is anticipated that 24 participants can
be recruited from Study 1 (n=50) with inclusion criteria of a grip strength <25th percentile.
Analysis
Effectiveness conclusions will not be drawn from this feasibility study, but exploratory comparative analysis will be
performed on endpoint data. Feasibility measures will be reported descriptively, including numbers eligible,
consented, randomised, completed, and also data completeness. All analysis will be conducted using SPSS
Statistics. All analyses will be performed on an intention-to-treat and per protocol basis. Analysis of the interviews
and focus group will use the five stage Framework Analysis methodology to deductively code and organise data
TFA40,52.

metabolic conferences.

will be at least two publications, one from the observational study and one from th

evidence.text:
the primary focus of this observational study as they were deemed most
important by our PPIE group. I have selected the following assessments with normative reference data that are
validated in physically active, adult populations23–26. With the exception of handgrip strength (measured in only
two PKU studies)19,21, none of these functional assessments have previously been measured in adults with PKU.
Handgrip strength – Widely recognised as a strong predictor of whole-body muscle strength27 and prognostic of
age-related health outcomes28.
Isokinetic dynamometry – This technique measures dynamic lower limb muscular contraction and is the gold
standard to assess muscle strength and power29.
Whilst isokinetic dynamometry is gold standard, it cannot be easily used in the clinical setting. Measurements by
isokinetic dynamometry will be compared to handgrip strength, a tool readily available in the clinical setting with
potential for widespread use in PKU following this study.
6-minute step test (6MST) - Exercise capacity is indirectly correlated with functional ability in healthy adults, and is
a key determinant of QoL and health outcomes30. The test will be performed on a single 20-cm

step in accordance
with the published protocol25.
Vertical jump test on force platform - The vertical jump test provides a measurement of lower limb muscular
power, an important physiological measure associated with the ability to perform activities of daily living and a
sensitive measurement of muscular change in healthy adults31. Vertical ground reaction force will also be
measured using a laboratory force plate, the gold standard for this measurement.
Anthropometry assessment
A range of gold standard and common clinical measures of anthropometry will be used. The correlation between
gold standard (e.g. dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)) vs. common clinical measures (e.g. bioelectrical
impedance analysis (BIA) and skinfolds) remain unknown in people with PKU, and can be measured here to
inform assessment approaches in clinical practice.
DXA - the gold standard measurement of lean mass, will be used to assess whole body and regional lower limb
lean mass. DXA measurements of lean mass closely correlate with measurements from MRI and CT, but at a
lower cost and with only small doses of radiation32.
BIA and skinfold thickness - these measurements provide estimates of fat-free m

ass and are common methods
for assessing body composition in a clinical setting. BIA has been validated in children and adolescents with
PKU32, but not adults2. Assessing fat-free mass in adults with PKU using these easily accessible techniques is
both practical and cost effective.
Height, weight, body mass index, and waist and thigh circumference will also be assessed.
Biochemical markers
Biochemical markers to assess protein status of people with PKU, including plasma amino acids, albumin,
prealbumin, total protein, retinol-binding protein and related biomarkers, will be measured. On test days, venous
blood samples (maximum of 20 ml) will be collected, processed, stored and analysed by the Clinical Biochemistry
laboratory at GSTT.
Metabolic control – Management of PKU in adults aims for blood phenylalanine concentrations between 120-600
µmol/L. Dried bloodspots (Guthrie cards) will be collected on waking on Day -7 and the test day, in line with
patients’ usual methods for self-monitoring.
Dietary assessment
Food diary and Food Frequency Questionnaire (FFQ)
A four-day food diary will be recorded and analysed by Nutritics software to determine current dietary protein
intake. In add

psychosocial factors, and the challenges and
barriers of adherence to a PKU-specific diet.
Other measurements – A questionnaire will be developed, and piloted with our PPIE group, to gather information
on diagnosis, PKU management (dietary and pharmacological) and adherence, and demographic characteristics
(e.g. age, sex, ethnicity) to ensure the study cohort is representative of the wider PKU population.
Data analysis
The following statistical analysis plan was developed in conjunction with KCL Statistical Support services. I will be
responsible for all data analysis with ongoing and extensive support from the KCL Statistical Services and my
supervisors.
Sample size
A sample size of 43 is required to detect differences in the primary measure (handgrip strength) in comparison to
healthy reference population data. This sample size is sufficient to detect a difference in mean grip force z-score
of -0.64 ± 1.26 (reported in Choukair et al, 2017) with 90% power and 5% significance from a two-sided one-
sample t-test (with the sample taken from a limited population of 4000 individuals with PKU in the UK).
Up to 50 participants will be recruited to obtain complete data for at least 43 pa

Study 2: Feasibility study
Study design
The feasibility study will be a RCT with two intervention arms conducted over 12-weeks (see Figure 2). A 12-week
duration is frequently used in studies investigating the impact of protein intake and/or exercise training on muscle
mass and functional outcomes38.
The intervention is a complex intervention with the interacting components of diet and exercise, that are behaviour
interventions39
dependent. Therefore, the MRC/NIHR framework for complex guided the development of this
research proposal, along with involvement from our PPIE group. The validated Theoretical Framework of
Acceptability (TFA) will be used to inform assessments of intervention acceptability40.
The feasibility of conducting a large RCT to assess the impact of protein intake on muscle mass and function in
adults with PKU is unknown. A feasibility study will be important to ascertain the acceptability and practicality of
the interventions, willingness to participate, retention rate, and to inform intervention development and delivery
from both the patients’ and healthcare professionals’ perspectives.

evidence.text:
cle mass and function and impact on quality of life
Our PKU PPIE group identified that adults with PKU are concerned about the impact of the PKU diet on functional
ability as they age. My nationwide questionnaire survey in 137 people with PKU identified that >50% have
concerns for their long-term health, echoing the issues raised by our PPIE group5. Maintenance of muscle mass is
crucial for supporting function, reducing cardiometabolic risk and frailty, and improving quality of life (QoL) with
advancing age16.
Following a very restrictive diet is a significant burden for people with PKU. In the UK, 73% of adults find PKU
dietary management difficult and 39% do not take their protein substitutes as prescribed12. Poor dietary
adherence has been reported to increase guilt and anxiety, thus negatively impacting health13. Research is
therefore needed to establish the minimum dietary protein intake that translates to optimal muscle mass and
function, without compromising phenylalanine control, to support healthy ageing in PKU.
Research is urgently needed to inform patient care
I surveyed Dietitians from nine UK-based metabolic centres and found varying protein prescribing practices. The

will be highly relevant to
my research area and will be targeted for future research funding.
Barriers to further research
An ongoing barrier to research in PKU is subject numbers due to the rare nature of the condition. However, 25%
of patients within our service have PKU, and with our patient numbers doubling every 5 years, I anticipate there
will be adequate numbers in our service alone to enable a large RCT.
Research Impact
Short-term (within 3 years): I plan to establish new care pathways for PKU clinical assessment. Incorporating
novel anthropometric and functional assessments will extend the scope of dietetic practice and allow patients to
receive more individualised care.
Medium-term (within 3-5 years): Outcomes of my research will inform the development of guidelines (in dietary
assessment, monitoring and intervention) that will in turn inform care pathways and practice standards for PKU
services nationally and internationally. Therefore, clinical practice will evolve to ensure optimal health outcomes
for PKU patients as they age. Furthermore, the feasibility study will inform the design of a full-scale clinical trial to
further investigate diet and lifestyle factors that

modulate muscle mass and function in adults with PKU.
Long-term (within 5+ years): With the revised guidelines, dietary assessment and monitoring of muscle mass
and function will be incorporated into routine clinical practice. With standardised assessment and monitoring,
multi-centre data can be collated, providing novel insight into the impact of PKU on health outcomes with ageing.
Outcomes from the full-scale clinical trial will inform further research into dietary advances to support ageing well
with PKU.
7. Project Management
The Project Advisory Group will consist of myself, my academic and practice supervisors, physiotherapist and
PPIE members. The group will meet at the beginning, mid-point and end of each study to discuss study
implementation, recruitment, retention, any issues raised during the study and progression against the agreed
timetable. In addition, the six core elements of the MRC/NIHR framework will be considered by the group to
inform how the research should proceed39. These meetings will be via video. Financial reimbursement has been
budgeted for three PPIE members’ involvement in these meetings. A diverse PPIE will be selected to ensure

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I founded the first Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT. This group has been pivotal in identifying research
priorities and informing this research proposal. In our first meeting in 2021, we discussed research priorities,
identifying that adults with PKU had concerns about the impact of PKU and its dietary management on long-term
review1, review2
health outcomes and functional ability in later life. This, along with my narrative scoping and my
nationwide survey of patients5, led to the development of the research questions. A recent PPIE meeting to revisit
the research proposal (based on feedback from my initial NIHR DCAF application), confirmed that this remains a
considerable research priority.
The research design evolved during multiple meetings with this PPIE group who informed the study design as
follows:
 Recruitment methods (suggesting screening and consent via video)
 Increasing test day attendance (offering compensation, written letters to request time off work and
provision of a suitable meal)
Test day assessment methods were r

evised based on members’ feedback. The PPIE group raised

concerns about some participants feeling self-conscious about their body composition and functional
assessment results and provided suggestions to overcome this potential issue (see ‘Ethics’)
 Ideas of written communication for the feasibility study, including a fridge calendar and journal to track
progress, and drop-in video sessions for participants to discuss any questions about the diet and exercise
interventions
 Reviewing of Plain English Summary (PES)
The Research Support Service also reviewed and provided feedback, as a non-PKU audience, on the PES and
research proposal.
Wider adult PKU community
I conceived, designed, conducted, analysed and published a nationwide questionnaire survey to examine the
knowledge, perceptions and dietary behaviours of adults with PKU5. Findings confirmed the importance of this
research in adults with PKU, with >50% having concerns for their long-term health, that are currently being unmet.
Up to 75% of people with PKU considered research focused on the impact of the PKU diet on muscle and health
and how regimens can be tailored to body composition and physical activity status, as eit

her extremely important
or very important, highlighting the value of this research.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I will continue my partnership with the Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT, who will be consulted regularly
throughout the fellowship.
This group brings lived experience, contributing valuable advice on engaging the PKU community to optimise
participation in research and ensuring that research meets the needs of this cohort.
The PKU PPIE group will be involved in, and advise on, the following during my fellowship:
 Advertising materials and methods to optimise recruitment
 Patient information sheet and consent forms to ensure clarity in the study and lay terminology so
participants can make an informed choice about participation
 Questionnaire and semi-structured interview design: questionnaires and interview questions designed by
the research team will be piloted with members of the PPIE group to test clarity and interpretation of
questions, readability, length and interview structure
 Dev

eloping participant information resources for the feasibility study (eg. the exercise guide, fridge
calendar and the journal). Members will advise on the design, layout and wording used for these resources
 Dissemination methods for communicating this research to the PKU community and other IMD services
Planning for further research (eg. post-doctoral funding)

Project Advisory Group
Three PPIE members will join the Project Advisory Group to support discussions on study implementation,
recruitment, retention and any issues raised during the study (see ‘Research Plan’). A diverse PPIE group will be
selected to ensure members can represent the views of the wider PKU community.
Supporting, enabling and capturing participation
Meetings will be held online to enable involvement from a wide geographical area. Financial reimbursement for the

Pre-doctoral Clinical Academic Fellowship (PCAF)
I was awarded a HEE/NIHR PCAF in 2019 that afforded me the opportunity to extend my research knowledge and
skills. I completed a PGCert in Clinical Research, including quantitative and qualitative research design, data
analysis and critical appraisal, and during this PCAF, published 4 first-author peer-reviewed full publications,
totaling 10 full publications in my NHS career (see ‘Publication Record’). I expanded my research skills by
undertaking the following initiatives:
Evidence review and synthesis
I completed the “Cochrane: Conducting an Intervention Review” online course, and attended several courses at
King’s College London (KCL) on systematic searches and writing literature reviews. I consolidated these skills
when I led on a narrative review and scoping review on protein requirements and protein status in PKU, both

ted. Following advice from our PPIE group, due to the large geographical area
covered by the service, appointments to discuss the study will be virtual (video/telephone). Eligible patients will be
sent a study information pack and participant consent will be returned via post. Additionally, to ensure equality of
access to participation, travel and time taken away from work or other duties will be reimbursed.
Inclusion criteria:
 Diagnosed with PKU on newborn screening
 Followed a stable management plan for ≥12 months
 Aged ≥18 years
 Able to walk independently up at least 1 flight of stairs
Exclusion criteria:
 Plasma phenylalanine concentration <600 µmol/L without treatment (mild PKU)
 Pregnant, lactating or pre-conception (as dietary management and metabolic control differs)
 Other co-morbidities that could influence protein intake and digestion
Data collection
The protocol is outlined in Figure 1. Participants will attend a test day at the Centre for Human and Applied
Physiological Sciences, King’s College London (KCL) for ~3 hours. I will perform all assessments outlined below,
and data will be entered into REDCap EDC.
Muscle function
Muscle function assessments will be

psychosocial factors, and the challenges and
barriers of adherence to a PKU-specific diet.
Other measurements – A questionnaire will be developed, and piloted with our PPIE group, to gather information
on diagnosis, PKU management (dietary and pharmacological) and adherence, and demographic characteristics
(e.g. age, sex, ethnicity) to ensure the study cohort is representative of the wider PKU population.
Data analysis
The following statistical analysis plan was developed in conjunction with KCL Statistical Support services. I will be
responsible for all data analysis with ongoing and extensive support from the KCL Statistical Services and my
supervisors.
Sample size
A sample size of 43 is required to detect differences in the primary measure (handgrip strength) in comparison to
healthy reference population data. This sample size is sufficient to detect a difference in mean grip force z-score
of -0.64 ± 1.26 (reported in Choukair et al, 2017) with 90% power and 5% significance from a two-sided one-
sample t-test (with the sample taken from a limited population of 4000 individuals with PKU in the UK).
Up to 50 participants will be recruited to obtain complete data for at least 43 pa

students through the Health Sciences Doctoral Training Centre (HSDTC) and the King’s Clinical Academic
Training Office (KCATO). Through the HSDTC and KCATO, I have selected courses in critical appraisal,
managing research data, academic writing, preparing for the upgrade, thesis writing, preparing for the viva, and

diseases (IMDs) services in England, I manage over 1000 patients with complex nutritional needs, including
phenylketonuria (PKU). Due to advances in neonatal screening and paediatric management, the number of
patients transitioning to adult services has increased exponentially. Despite diet being the mainstay of treatment,
there is limited evidence informing the dietary management of adults with PKU, and other IMDs, creating an urgent

techniques are novel in IMD and would allow for more individualised assessment and tailored dietary interventions.
Maintaining my involvement in these clinics will ensure that these developments translate into improved patient
care.
With the NHS advancing use of virtual patient consults, GSTT IMD service has adopted patient webinars to deliver
updates in clinical management. I will contribute to the development, updating and running of webinars and
provide specialist guidance for complex questions regarding dietary management of IMDs.
International Clinical Advisor
I will continue my role on the Advisory Board for the Global DARE Foundation including annual revision and
publication of dietary guidance, hosting training webinars, and providing ongoing clinical expertise to Dietitians
internationally.

Name Dr Oliver Witard
Institution King’s College London
Position Senior Lecturer in Exercise Metabolism & Nutrition
Description of support provided
Dr Oliver Witard will be my Academic Supervisor. His research explores the interaction of exercise and nutritional
interventions, primarily protein

nutrition, to offset the age-related decline in muscle mass and function. His
expertise is therefore directly aligned with my research proposal.

and is currently supervising one PhD as primary, and another as secondary, supervisor. During my NIHR PCAF, I
collaborated with Dr Witard on several research activities, culminating in the publications of a narrative review and
scoping review on protein requirements and protein status in people with PKU. Dr Witard has been involved
throughout in developing this doctoral research proposal, providing vital knowledge and insight to the research
design and the proposed functional measurements.
Dr Witard has extensive experience in the management of human trials in protein nutrition and muscle metabolism
research, including the assessments of muscle functional capacity. He has led on multiple studies investigating
nutritional and exercise interventions in young and older adults with the primary outcome targeted at optimising
skeletal muscle mass and function. His expertise will be invaluable during my fellowship and by informing the
des

Dr Radha Ramachandran will be my Practice and 3rd Academic Supervisor. She was appointed Consultant in
Metabolic Medicine in 2013. Dr Ramachandran chairs the UK Adult Inherited Metabolic Diseases (IMD)
Physicians’ Group and sits on the British IMD Group Clinical Guidelines and NICE Diagnostic Accuracy
Committees. She has international expertise in, and sits on Advisory Boards for, ORPHAN lipid and peroxisomal
disorders.

Chemical Pathology and Metabolic Medicine at Imperial College Healthca

Given the clinical mentorship she has provided thus far, our shared clinical and research interests and the need for
a multidisciplinary approach to managing patients with IMD, having Dr Ramachandran as my Practice and
Academic Supervisor will provide me with the support needed to further develop my career as a clinical academic
in IMD.

evidence.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I founded the first Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT. This group has been pivotal in identifying research
priorities and informing this research proposal. In our first meeting in 2021, we discussed research priorities,
identifying that adults with PKU had concerns about the impact of PKU and its dietary management on long-term
review1, review2
health outcomes and functional ability in later life. This, along with my narrative scoping and my
nationwide survey of patients5, led to the development of the research questions. A recent PPIE meeting to revisit
the research proposal (based on feedback from my initial NIHR DCAF application), confirmed that this remains a
considerable research priority.
The research design evolved during multiple meetings with this PPIE group who informed the study design as
follows:
 Recruitment methods (suggesting screening and consent via video)
 Increasing test day attendance (offering compensation, written letters to request time off work and
provision of a suitable meal)
Test day assessment methods were r

evised based on members’ feedback. The PPIE group raised

concerns about some participants feeling self-conscious about their body composition and functional
assessment results and provided suggestions to overcome this potential issue (see ‘Ethics’)
 Ideas of written communication for the feasibility study, including a fridge calendar and journal to track
progress, and drop-in video sessions for participants to discuss any questions about the diet and exercise
interventions
 Reviewing of Plain English Summary (PES)
The Research Support Service also reviewed and provided feedback, as a non-PKU audience, on the PES and
research proposal.
Wider adult PKU community
I conceived, designed, conducted, analysed and published a nationwide questionnaire survey to examine the
knowledge, perceptions and dietary behaviours of adults with PKU5. Findings confirmed the importance of this
research in adults with PKU, with >50% having concerns for their long-term health, that are currently being unmet.
Up to 75% of people with PKU considered research focused on the impact of the PKU diet on muscle and health
and how regimens can be tailored to body composition and physical activity status, as eit

her extremely important
or very important, highlighting the value of this research.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I will continue my partnership with the Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT, who will be consulted regularly
throughout the fellowship.
This group brings lived experience, contributing valuable advice on engaging the PKU community to optimise
participation in research and ensuring that research meets the needs of this cohort.
The PKU PPIE group will be involved in, and advise on, the following during my fellowship:
 Advertising materials and methods to optimise recruitment
 Patient information sheet and consent forms to ensure clarity in the study and lay terminology so
participants can make an informed choice about participation
 Questionnaire and semi-structured interview design: questionnaires and interview questions designed by
the research team will be piloted with members of the PPIE group to test clarity and interpretation of
questions, readability, length and interview structure
 Dev

eloping participant information resources for the feasibility study (eg. the exercise guide, fridge
calendar and the journal). Members will advise on the design, layout and wording used for these resources
 Dissemination methods for communicating this research to the PKU community and other IMD services
Planning for further research (eg. post-doctoral funding)

Project Advisory Group
Three PPIE members will join the Project Advisory Group to support discussions on study implementation,
recruitment, retention and any issues raised during the study (see ‘Research Plan’). A diverse PPIE group will be
selected to ensure members can represent the views of the wider PKU community.
Supporting, enabling and capturing participation
Meetings will be held online to enable involvement from a wide geographical area. Financial reimbursement for the
above consultations has been costed into this fellowship to compensate for PPIEs’ time and active involvement,
calculated using the NIHR payment guidance. New members to the group will be offered training in PPIE through
NIHR Biomedical Research Centre at GSTT (or similar) to ensure they feel equipped and confident to contribute to
these meetings. F

(e.g. US vs DXA, FFQ vs food diary). All will be published open access to ensure wide dissemination.
 Healthcare innovation: My research will help to establish reliable markers of protein status to
incorporate into the clinical assessment and monitoring of patients with PKU. This will advance current
clinical practice in determining dietary requirements and monitoring the outcomes of dietary interventions.
Research dissemination to patients and professionals
I am keen to disseminate my findings to the patients who may benefit from it. Our PPIE group will be actively
involved in disseminating this research. During the first year of my Fellowship, I will work with our PKU PPIE
group to develop plans for informing service users and other NHS services nationwide of the research outputs,
and engage funding bodies to support future research.
The National Society of PKU (NSPKU, patient support charity) are key stakeholders in disseminating research to
national and international PKU communities via their website, social media and annual conference. I will
collab

orate with NSPKU to disseminate my research via these platforms.
The BIMDG specialist group consists of clinicians, clinical service leads and national advisors. I plan to present
my research at BIMDG conferences and group meetings to inform and engage key stakeholders influencing NHS
service provision for patients with PKU.
Impact on the health system (guidelines)
I intend to contribute to the review and development of dietary management guidelines informing clinical practice
2017)9
in the UK and Europe, such as in the ESPKU guidelines (last published and the UK consensus on Dietetic
Management of Adults with PKU (published 2022)3. These guidelines are reviewed every 3-5 years. My research
will contribute to the evidence informing protein requirement guidelines, dietary interventions, and assessment
and monitoring of protein status in adults with PKU.
Further research funding
I plan to develop a proposal for post-doctoral research through the NIHR ACAF scheme to undertake a full-scale
complex intervention trial to investigate the impact of dietary regimens and lifestyle factors to optimise muscle
mass and function in adults with PKU. In addition, other NIHR and MRC research grants

will be highly relevant to
my research area and will be targeted for future research funding.
Barriers to further research
An ongoing barrier to research in PKU is subject numbers due to the rare nature of the condition. However, 25%
of patients within our service have PKU, and with our patient numbers doubling every 5 years, I anticipate there
will be adequate numbers in our service alone to enable a large RCT.
Research Impact
Short-term (within 3 years): I plan to establish new care pathways for PKU clinical assessment. Incorporating
novel anthropometric and functional assessments will extend the scope of dietetic practice and allow patients to
receive more individualised care.
Medium-term (within 3-5 years): Outcomes of my research will inform the development of guidelines (in dietary
assessment, monitoring and intervention) that will in turn inform care pathways and practice standards for PKU
services nationally and internationally. Therefore, clinical practice will evolve to ensure optimal health outcomes
for PKU patients as they age. Furthermore, the feasibility study will inform the design of a full-scale clinical trial to

evidence.text:
Background
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a rare inherited metabolic disorder of protein metabolism with a prevalence of 1:10,000
in the UK. Left untreated, high serum phenylalanine causes significant irreversible neurocognitive disability.
Dietary management remains the mainstay of treatment, consisting of lifelong adherence to a very low protein diet
(typically <15 grams per day) and provision of up to 80% of protein intake from phenylalanine-free or low-
phenylalanine protein substitutes9.
1. Problems being addressed
Establishing optimal protein intake in adults with PKU: “Not too little, not too much”
With the onset of newborn screening for PKU in 1969, there is now an increasing adult and ageing PKU
population requiring dietary guidance to support changing metabolic demands across the lifespan. In my narrative
review, I highlighted that there is currently limited evidence informing protein requirement guidelines for adults
with PKU1.
Current protein intake guidance for adults with PKU is extrapolated from healthy population data, using estimates
of protein requirements based on either nitrogen balance (NB) methodology or from younger adults with PKU1.
The limitations associated with

protein requirements (0.83g/kg/day)
(standard current practice) vs. IAAO-informed protein requirements (1.2g/kg/day) on muscle mass and function in
adults with PKU. I aim to establish protein requirements for adults with PKU by performing a feasibility study (as
part of my PhD) and then undertaking a larger RCT (as part of a NIHR ACAF).
4. Research aims and objectives
This research proposal aims to:
(i) assess muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters of protein status in adults with PKU;
(ii) investigate whether impairments in muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters in adults with
PKU are associated with PKU dietary management practices;
(iii) examine the feasibility of a study investigating the comparison of protein intake informed by NB (0.83g/kg/day)
vs. IAAO (1.2g/kg/day) and its impact on muscle mass and function, and phenylalanine control in adults with PKU.
Objectives
I propose to undertake two studies:
Study 1: Observational study
 Primary objective: To investigate differences in muscle mass and function (anthropometric, functional and
biochemical measurements) in adults with PKU vs. reference healthy population dataset.
Secondary objective:

To investigate associations between dietary factors (protein intake, protein

substitute use), muscle mass and physical activity, and muscle function in adults with PKU.
Study 2: Feasibility study:
 To determine the impact of increasing prescribed protein intake and exercise training on muscle mass
and function in adults with PKU who have compromised muscle strength.
 To establish the feasibility of implementing this complex intervention over 12 weeks in adults with PKU,
with the view to designing a full-scale RCT.
5. Project Plan
Study 1: Observational study
Study design
A cross-sectional, observational study will be conducted over an 18-month period in adults with PKU enrolled from
the Adult Metabolic Service at GSTT.
An observational study is critical to understanding the association between current dietary management practices
on muscle mass and function in adults with PKU and to establish the most robust endpoints for use in future trials.
The proposed research will be the first to simultaneously measure anthropometric, functional and biochemical
parameters of protein status in an adult UK cohort with PKU.
